text: This film shows a minimally invasive surgical approach for a thymectomy in patients with myasthenia gravis. The surgical technique applied is a robot-assisted videothoracoscopy in which we perform a thymectomy and remove the anterior mediastinal fat, adopting a left transpleuric approach. The chest X-ray is important to verify the feasibility of the transpleuric approach. The X-ray does not show any evident pleuro parenchymal inflammation. The CT scan shows a hyperplasia of the thymus and excludes any tumoral pathology.

The patient is positioned with a double lumen endotracheal tube at a thirty-degree angle, using a shoulder roll and bean bag for support. The mammary gland is dislocated towards the center for improved access. The surgeon identifies intercostal spaces from the eleventh to the fifth, where thoracoscopic ports are placed. The skin is dissected at the anterior axillary line for two centimeters. The pleural space is manually tested, lung ventilation is interrupted, and a trocar connected to CO₂ insufflation is introduced for camera insertion. The endothoracic view reveals port sites at the fifth intercostal space in the midclavicular line and the third intercostal space in the midaxillary line.

The robotic system is positioned at a forty-five-degree angle to the patient's axis. The surgeon explores the mediastinic surface of the left pleural space, identifying the pericardium, phrenic nerve, and mammary vessels. The procedure begins with the removal of fat tissue from the cardiophrenic angle. An incision is made from the bottom to the top of the retrosternal mediastinal pleura, and the thymic tissue is dissected from the retrosternal space. The thymus is isolated from the pericardic surface in a bottom-to-top manner.

The surgeon makes a pleural incision at the apical level to access the area containing the mammary vessels and the phrenic nerve. The thymus gland is carefully mobilized from the bottom to the top, ensuring the phrenic nerve is not injured during the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the cervical poles in the neck region, carefully isolating the inominate veins. Downward traction is applied to the left cervical pole, allowing visualization of the thymic vein branches, which are doubly clipped and transected. The dissection of thymic tissue from the right mediastinal pleura is completed, with caution taken to avoid injury to the right phrenic nerve.

An endobag is introduced through the inferior port into the pleural space, and the thymus is placed inside it for removal. A chest tube is inserted, and the lung is reinflated, with both maneuvers visually confirmed using the optical view.
